text: We therefore did proceed with a thoracoscopic left upper lobectomy with the hopes of minimizing the morbidity in this young patient.
As we can see, the upper lobe parenchyma was stuck to the phrenic bundle anteriorly and the dissection was relatively tedious in order to avoid an injury to the phrenic nerve.
We were then able to expose the anterior hilum and both the lingular vein and the left upper lobe vein proper.
Having a 4x4 sponge in the chest, we were able to sequentially go around the lingular vein and superior pulmonary vein proper.
Our goal was to divide both veins early on in order to avoid any intraoperative embolizations.
As shown, we used a vascular thickness endogia stapler for both veins.
Going around the superior pulmonary vein proper was slightly more delicate.
After dividing the veins, we turned our attention to the major fissure and were able to go around one of the apical PA branches to the upper lobe.
We continued the dissection in the major fissure in order to go around its inferior portion.
The major fissure was completed using an endo-GIA medium thickness stapler, as you can see.
We then divided the apical PA branch posteriorly.
We turned our attention back to the anterior hilum in order to go around the anterior apical PA branches.
A hard and enlarged lymph node was encountered and had to be dissected first.
Division of the anterior apical PA branch was performed with an endogia vascular thickness stapler.
We were then left with a left upper lobe bronchus, which was divided uneventfully with a medium thickness endogia stapler.
The specimen was removed using a retrieval bag.
